---
title: "Neurologic Injury Profiles in Atrial Fibrillation Ablation"
subtitle: "A Quarto + Typst academic poster example with brand colors, cards, callouts, and figures"
author:
  - name: "Ada Lovelace"
    affiliation: "Department of Computational Methods"
  - name: "Alan Turing"
    affiliation: "Institute for Mathematical Research"
brand: _brand.yml
format:
  academic-poster-typst:
    poster-width: 56in
    poster-height: 31.5in
    margin:
      x: 0.55in
      y: 0.45in
    fontsize: 18pt
    poster-column-gap: 0.55in
    poster-column-widths: [0.95fr, 1.35fr, 0.95fr]
    poster-header-height: 15%
    poster-footer-height: 6%
    poster-row-gap: 0.35in
    poster-corner-radius: 7pt
    poster-feature-inset: 0.32in
    poster-title-size: 1.42em
    poster-subtitle-size: 0.66em
    poster-author-size: 0.52em
    poster-institution-size: 0.46em
    poster-h1-size: 0.78em
    poster-h2-size: 0.72em
    poster-h3-size: 0.68em
    poster-card-body-inset: "(x: 0.42em, y: 0.28em)"
    poster-card-gap: 0.26em
    poster-callout-size: 0.78em
    poster-callout-large-size: 0.9em
    footer-left: "Scan for preprint"
    footer-center: "Heart Rhythm Scientific Sessions 2026"
    footer-right: "#HRS2026"
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
library(ggplot2)

brand <- list(
  primary = "#b01c32",
  secondary = "#6f1020",
  blush = "#f8e8eb",
  ink = "#17212b"
)

events <- data.frame(
  year = rep(2021:2026, each = 3),
  energy = rep(c("PFA", "RFA", "Cryo"), times = 6),
  reports = c(0, 82, 51, 0, 91, 58, 3, 105, 62, 26, 112, 67, 94, 119, 71, 128, 123, 73)
)

proportions <- data.frame(
  energy = c("PFA", "RFA", "Cryo"),
  neurologic = c(2.9, 1.1, 0.9),
  lower = c(2.3, 0.8, 0.6),
  upper = c(3.6, 1.5, 1.3)
)

modality_colors <- c(PFA = brand$primary, RFA = "#4b5563", Cryo = "#9ca3af")

theme_poster <- function(base_size = 11) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = brand$secondary),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(color = brand$ink),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
      axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
      legend.position = "bottom",
      legend.title = element_blank(),
      plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
      panel.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA)
    )
}

dir.create("figures", showWarnings = FALSE)

p_events <- ggplot(events, aes(year, reports, color = energy, group = energy)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 2.8) +
  scale_color_manual(values = modality_colors) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2021:2026) +
  labs(
    title = "Reported neurologic events by ablation modality",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Reports"
  ) +
  theme_poster()

p_proportions <- ggplot(proportions, aes(energy, neurologic, fill = energy)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.62) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), width = 0.18, linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = modality_colors) +
  labs(
    title = "Neurologic event proportion among modality reports",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Neurologic reports (%)"
  ) +
  theme_poster() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

ggsave("figures/reported-events.png", p_events, width = 7.6, height = 2.8, dpi = 200, bg = "white")
ggsave("figures/event-proportions.png", p_proportions, width = 7.6, height = 2.7, dpi = 200, bg = "white")
```

::: {.poster-column}

# Background {.compact}

Pulsed field ablation (PFA) is a nonthermal strategy for atrial fibrillation ablation. Its theoretical advantages include myocardial selectivity and reduced collateral injury, but post-market neurologic safety signals remain incompletely characterized.

# Objective {.compact}

Compare the profile of neurologic adverse event reports across PFA, radiofrequency ablation (RFA), and cryoablation using a reproducible poster workflow.

# Methods {.compact}

- Simulated adverse event report counts by year and ablation modality.
- Events were grouped into symptomatic neurologic and embolic categories.
- Report profiles were summarized before and after the introduction of PFA.
- The poster uses ordinary Markdown sections plus layout classes.

# Notes {.compact .light}

This example is intentionally synthetic. It demonstrates layout, figure placement, and theming rather than making scientific claims.

:::

::: {.poster-column .poster-feature}

# Key Findings {.compact .inverse}

::: {.poster-callout .large}
Neurologic reports increase after PFA adoption in this simulated surveillance dataset.
:::

![](figures/reported-events.png){width=100%}

::: {.poster-callout .light}
The central `.poster-feature` column behaves like a revealjs slide modifier: add a class and the panel changes without touching Typst.
:::

![](figures/event-proportions.png){width=100%}

::: {.poster-callout .secondary}
Poster-wide options such as `poster-h1-size`, `poster-h2-size`, `poster-card-gap`, and `poster-callout-size` control typography and spacing for the whole document.
:::

:::

::: {.poster-column}

# Results {.compact}

| Modality | Reports | Neurologic % |
|---|---:|---:|
| PFA | 8,787 | 2.9 |
| RFA | 22,761 | 1.1 |
| Cryo | 5,188 | 0.9 |

The simulated PFA series shows a higher proportion of neurologic reports than thermal energy modalities. The example uses simple generated data so that the poster can be rendered and modified locally.

# Discussion {.compact}

The major design pattern is intentionally close to revealjs:

- Add `.poster-column` to create an explicit layout region.
- Add `.poster-feature` for a high-contrast results panel.
- Add `.poster-callout` for short emphasized claims.
- Add heading classes like `.compact`, `.light`, `.secondary`, or `.plain` to adjust cards.

# Takeaway {.compact .secondary}

Quarto metadata, `_brand.yml` colors, executable figures, and simple Markdown classes can drive a poster that remains editable as plain text.

:::
